1→---
     2→title: "Two Modes of Operation"
     3→description: "Sequant offers two ways to work: interactive in Claude Code chat, or autonomous via headless CLI."
     4→draft: false
     5→---
     6→
     7→Sequant offers two ways to work: interactive in Claude Code chat, or autonomous via headless CLI.
     8→
     9→## Mode Comparison
    10→
    11→| Aspect | Interactive | Autonomous |
    12→|--------|-------------|------------|
    13→| **Interface** | Claude Code chat | Terminal CLI |
    14→| **Control** | Review each phase | Hands-off |
    15→| **Best for** | Complex issues, learning | Batch processing |
    16→| **Iteration** | Manual `/loop` | Automatic quality loop |
    17→
    18→## Interactive Mode
    19→
    20→Use slash commands in Claude Code chat for step-by-step control.
    21→
    22→### Commands
    23→
    24→```bash
    25→/spec 123      # Plan implementation
    26→/exec 123      # Build in worktree
    27→/test 123      # UI verification
    28→/qa 123        # Quality review
    29→/loop 123      # Fix and retry
    30→
    31→# Or all-in-one
    32→/fullsolve 123
    33→```
    34→
    35→### When to Use
    36→
    37→- **Learning Sequant** — See each phase's output
    38→- **Complex issues** — Review plans before implementation
    39→- **Sensitive changes** — Need human oversight at each step
    40→- **Debugging** — Investigate issues between phases
    41→
    42→### Example Session
    43→
    44→```
    45→You: /spec 42
    46→
    47→Claude: I've analyzed issue #42 and drafted a plan...
    48→[Shows implementation plan]
    49→
    50→You: Looks good, but let's use a different approach for the database query.
    51→
    52→Claude: Updated plan. Ready for /exec?
    53→
    54→You: /exec 42
    55→
    56→Claude: Implemented in worktree feature/42-add-caching...
    57→[Shows changes and test results]
    58→
    59→You: /qa 42
    60→
    61→Claude: QA complete. All AC items satisfied. Ready to merge.
    62→```
    63→
    64→### Advantages
    65→
    66→- Full visibility into each phase
    67→- Can intervene and redirect
    68→- Better for learning and understanding
    69→- Collaborative decision-making
    70→
    71→## Autonomous Mode
    72→
    73→Use the CLI for batch processing without interaction.
    74→
    75→### Commands
    76→
    77→```bash
    78→# Single issue
    79→npx sequant run 123
    80→
    81→# Multiple issues in parallel
    82→npx sequant run 1 2 3
    83→
    84→# Sequential processing
    85→npx sequant run 1 2 3 --sequential
    86→
    87→# With quality loop (auto-fix)
    88→npx sequant run 123 --quality-loop
    89→
    90→# Custom phases
    91→npx sequant run 123 --phases exec,qa
    92→
    93→# Preview without execution
    94→npx sequant run 123 --dry-run
    95→```
    96→
    97→### When to Use
    98→
    99→- **Batch processing** — Multiple issues at once
   100→- **CI/CD integration** — Automated workflows
   101→- **Simple issues** — Bug fixes, docs updates
   102→- **Overnight runs** — Queue work and review later
   103→
   104→### Example: Batch Processing
   105→
   106→```bash
   107→# Process sprint backlog overnight
   108→npx sequant run 45 46 47 48 --quality-loop
   109→
   110→# In the morning, review PRs
   111→gh pr list
   112→```
   113→
   114→### Quality Loop
   115→
   116→Autonomous mode can automatically fix issues:
   117→
   118→```bash
   119→npx sequant run 123 --quality-loop --max-iterations 5
   120→```
   121→
   122→**How it works:**
   123→1. Runs phases (spec → exec → qa)
   124→2. If QA fails, runs `/loop` to fix
   125→3. Re-runs failed phases
   126→4. Repeats up to N iterations
   127→
   128→### Smart Defaults
   129→
   130→Quality loop auto-enables for issues with these labels:
   131→- `complex`
   132→- `refactor`
   133→- `breaking`
   134→- `major`
   135→
   136→### Advantages
   137→
   138→- Process multiple issues efficiently
   139→- No manual intervention needed
   140→- Consistent execution
   141→- Good for overnight batch work
   142→
   143→## Hybrid Workflow
   144→
   145→Combine modes for the best of both:
   146→
   147→```bash
   148→# Start with interactive spec
   149→/spec 123
   150→# Review and approve plan
   151→
   152→# Run implementation autonomously
   153→npx sequant run 123 --phases exec,qa --quality-loop
   154→
   155→# Review results in chat
   156→/assess 123
   157→```
   158→
   159→## Mode Selection Guide
   160→
   161→| Scenario | Recommended Mode |
   162→|----------|------------------|
   163→| First time using Sequant | Interactive |
   164→| Complex feature with unclear requirements | Interactive |
   165→| Simple bug fix | Autonomous |
   166→| Sprint batch processing | Autonomous |
   167→| Security-sensitive change | Interactive |
   168→| Documentation updates | Autonomous |
   169→| Refactoring with breaking changes | Interactive (spec), then autonomous |
   170→
   171→## Configuration
   172→
   173→### Default Settings
   174→
   175→Configure in `.sequant/settings.json`:
   176→
   177→```json
   178→{
   179→  "run": {
   180→    "qualityLoop": false,
   181→    "maxIterations": 3,
   182→    "sequential": false
   183→  }
   184→}
   185→```
   186→
   187→### Override Per Run
   188→
   189→```bash
   190→# Override settings with flags
   191→npx sequant run 123 --quality-loop --max-iterations 5 --sequential
   192→```
   193→
   194→CLI flags always override settings file values.
   195→

1→---
     2→title: "Sequant Cheat Sheet"
     3→draft: false
     4→---
     5→
     6→> Quick reference for all commands, flags, and common workflows.
     7→> For detailed docs, see the [full documentation](https://github.com/sequant-io/sequant/blob/main/docs/README.md).
     8→
     9→---
    10→
    11→## Installation & Setup
    12→
    13→```bash
    14→# Install globally
    15→npm install -g sequant
    16→
    17→# Or run via npx (no install needed)
    18→npx sequant init
    19→```
    20→
    21→#### Update & sync
    22→
    23→```bash
    24→sequant update              # Update skill templates to latest version
    25→sequant sync                # Sync skills and templates (non-interactive, CI-safe)
    26→sequant doctor              # Verify installation health
    27→```
    28→
    29→#### When to use which
    30→
    31→| Scenario | Command |
    32→|----------|---------|
    33→| First time setup | `sequant init` |
    34→| New Sequant version released | `npm update -g sequant && sequant update` |
    35→| CI/automation template sync | `sequant sync` |
    36→| Something seems off | `sequant doctor` |
    37→
    38→---
    39→
    40→## Quick Start
    41→
    42→| Goal | Command |
    43→|------|---------|
    44→| Not sure where to start? | `/solve 123` |
    45→| Solve an issue end-to-end | `/fullsolve 123` |
    46→| Step-by-step workflow | `/spec 123` → `/exec 123` → `/qa 123` |
    47→| Headless batch run | `npx sequant run 1 2 3 --quality-loop` |
    48→
    49→---
    50→
    51→## Slash Commands
    52→
    53→**Core Workflow**
    54→
    55→| Command | Purpose |
    56→|---------|---------|
    57→| `/spec <issue>` | Plan and review acceptance criteria, post issue comment |
    58→| `/exec <issue>` | Build in isolated worktree until AC met |
    59→| `/test <issue>` | Browser-based testing for admin features (needs Chrome DevTools MCP) |
    60→| `/qa <issue>` | Code review and quality gate validation |
    61→
    62→**Automation**
    63→
    64→| Command | Purpose |
    65→|---------|---------|
    66→| `/fullsolve <issue>` | Full pipeline: spec → exec → test → qa with auto-fix |
    67→| `/solve <issues...>` | Recommend optimal workflow for issue(s) |
    68→| `/loop` | Re-run quality checks, fix failures, iterate |
    69→
    70→**Testing & Docs**
    71→
    72→| Command | Purpose |
    73→|---------|---------|
    74→| `/testgen <issue>` | Generate test stubs from spec verification criteria |
    75→| `/verify` | CLI/script execution verification with captured output |
    76→| `/docs <issue>` | Generate admin-facing feature documentation |
    77→
    78→**Analysis & Utilities**
    79→
    80→| Command | Purpose |
    81→|---------|---------|
    82→| `/assess <issue>` | Triage issue, recommend next phase |
    83→| `/improve` | Discover codebase improvements, create issues |
    84→| `/reflect` | Analyze workflow effectiveness |
    85→| `/security-review` | Deep security analysis for sensitive features |
    86→| `/clean` | Repository cleanup (stale worktrees, branches) |
    87→| `/merger` | Multi-issue post-QA merge coordination |
    88→| `/upstream` | Monitor Claude Code releases for compatibility |
    89→| `/release` | Version bump, tag, GitHub release, npm publish |
    90→
    91→---
    92→
    93→## CLI Commands
    94→
    95→| Command | Purpose |
    96→|---------|---------|
    97→| `sequant init` | Initialize Sequant in a project |
    98→| `sequant update` | Update skill templates to latest versions |
    99→| `sequant sync` | Sync skills and templates (non-interactive) |
   100→| `sequant doctor` | Check installation health and prerequisites |
   101→| `sequant status [issue]` | Show version, config, workflow state |
   102→| `sequant run <issues...>` | Execute workflow headlessly |
   103→| `sequant state [init\|rebuild\|clean]` | Manage workflow state |
   104→| `sequant stats` | View local workflow analytics |
   105→| `sequant dashboard` | Launch real-time workflow dashboard |
   106→| `sequant conventions` | View, detect, or reset codebase conventions |
   107→| `sequant logs` | View and manage log files |
   108→
   109→---
   110→
   111→## `sequant run` Flags
   112→
   113→**Execution Mode**
   114→
   115→| Flag | Description | Default |
   116→|------|-------------|---------|
   117→| `--sequential` | Run issues one at a time | off (parallel) |
   118→| `--chain` | Chain issues: each branches from previous (implies `--sequential`) | off |
   119→| `--qa-gate` | Wait for QA pass before next issue in chain (requires `--chain`) | off |
   120→| `--phases <list>` | Phases to run | `spec,exec,qa` |
   121→| `--resume` | Resume from last completed phase (reads GitHub markers) | off |
   122→| `--base <branch>` | Base branch for worktree creation | `main` (or `settings.run.defaultBase`) |
   123→
   124→**Quality**
   125→
   126→| Flag | Description | Default |
   127→|------|-------------|---------|
   128→| `-q, --quality-loop` | Auto-retry on failures | off |
   129→| `--max-iterations <n>` | Max retry iterations | `3` |
   130→| `--testgen` | Run testgen phase after spec | off |
   131→| `--smart-tests` | Enable smart test detection | on |
   132→| `--no-smart-tests` | Disable smart test detection | — |
   133→
   134→**Environment**
   135→
   136→| Flag | Description | Default |
   137→|------|-------------|---------|
   138→| `--no-mcp` | Disable MCP server injection | off |
   139→| `--timeout <seconds>` | Timeout per phase in seconds | `1800` |
   140→| `--batch <issues>` | Group issues to run together (e.g., `--batch "1 2"`) | `[]` |
   141→
   142→**Output**
   143→
   144→| Flag | Description | Default |
   145→|------|-------------|---------|
   146→| `-d, --dry-run` | Preview without execution | off |
   147→| `-v, --verbose` | Verbose output with streaming | off |
   148→| `--log-json` | Enable structured JSON logging | on |
   149→| `--no-log` | Disable JSON logging for this run | — |
   150→| `--log-path <path>` | Custom log directory path | `.sequant/logs/` |
   151→| `--quiet` | Suppress version warnings and non-essential output | off |
   152→
   153→---
   154→
   155→## Common Workflows
   156→
   157→#### Single issue, full auto
   158→```bash
   159→/fullsolve 123
   160→```
   161→Runs spec → exec → test → qa with automatic fix loops.
   162→
   163→#### Step-by-step with control
   164→```bash
   165→/spec 123        # Review the plan
   166→/exec 123        # Implement in worktree
   167→/qa 123          # Quality gate check
   168→```
   169→Pause between phases to review output and adjust.
   170→
   171→#### Batch three issues
   172→```bash
   173→npx sequant run 101 102 103 --quality-loop
   174→```
   175→Runs all issues with auto-retry on failures.
   176→
   177→#### Sequential chain (each builds on previous)
   178→```bash
   179→npx sequant run 101 102 103 --sequential --chain --qa-gate
   180→```
   181→Each issue branches from the last; waits for QA pass before continuing.
   182→
   183→#### Resume a failed run
   184→```bash
   185→npx sequant run 123 --resume
   186→```
   187→Picks up from the last completed phase using GitHub markers.
   188→
   189→#### Dry run to preview
   190→```bash
   191→npx sequant run 101 102 --dry-run
   192→```
   193→Shows what would happen without executing anything.
   194→
   195→#### Generate tests before implementation
   196→```bash
   197→npx sequant run 123 --phases spec,testgen,exec,qa
   198→```
   199→Creates test stubs from spec, then implements against them.
   200→
   201→#### Security-sensitive feature
   202→```bash
   203→/spec 123
   204→/exec 123
   205→/security-review
   206→/qa 123
   207→```
   208→Adds a security review phase before final QA.
   209→
   210→---
   211→
   212→## Phase Verdicts
   213→
   214→| Verdict | Meaning | Action |
   215→|---------|---------|--------|
   216→| `READY_FOR_MERGE` | All AC met, quality high | Merge the PR |
   217→| `AC_MET_BUT_NOT_A_PLUS` | AC met, meaningful improvements suggested | Merge or iterate |
   218→| `NEEDS_VERIFICATION` | AC met, pending external verification | Verify in CI/staging |
   219→| `AC_NOT_MET` | Acceptance criteria failures | Fix and re-run `/qa` |
   220→
   221→---
   222→
   223→## Key File Locations
   224→
   225→```
   226→.claude/skills/          # Skill definitions (slash commands)
   227→.claude/settings.json    # Claude Code settings
   228→.sequant/settings.json   # Sequant configuration
   229→.sequant/conventions.json # Detected codebase conventions
   230→.sequant/state.json      # Workflow state tracking
   231→.sequant/metrics.json    # Local analytics data
   232→.sequant/logs/           # Run logs
   233→worktrees/               # Isolated git worktrees per issue
   234→```
   235→
   236→---
   237→
   238→## Troubleshooting
   239→
   240→| Problem | Fix |
   241→|---------|-----|
   242→| Something feels broken | `npx sequant doctor` |
   243→| Check current state | `npx sequant status` |
   244→| View recent logs | `npx sequant logs` |
   245→| Clean up stale worktrees | `/clean` |
   246→| Reset workflow state | `npx sequant state clean` |

1→---
     2→title: "What Is Sequant"
     3→description: "Sequant is workflow automation for Claude Code. It turns GitHub issues into merge-ready pull requests through structured phases and quality gates."
     4→draft: false
     5→---
     6→
     7→Sequant is workflow automation for Claude Code. It turns GitHub issues into
     8→merge-ready pull requests through structured phases and quality gates.
     9→
    10→You write issues. Sequant plans, builds, tests, and reviews them — each step
    11→with guardrails that catch problems before they reach your codebase.
    12→
    13→---
    14→
    15→## The Problem
    16→
    17→Claude Code is remarkably capable. Give it a well-scoped issue and it can
    18→plan, implement, and test a feature in minutes. But capability alone doesn't
    19→guarantee consistency.
    20→
    21→Without structure, AI-assisted development has a "results may vary" quality:
    22→
    23→- A vague issue produces a vague implementation
    24→- There's no formal review step — code goes from "written" to "done"
    25→- Context accumulates over long sessions, drifting from the original goal
    26→- There's nothing tracking which issues are planned, in progress, or reviewed
    27→- Each session starts from scratch with no memory of prior work
    28→
    29→These aren't flaws in Claude Code. They're the natural consequence of
    30→working without a process. Human developers solve this with methodologies,
    31→code review, CI/CD, and project management. AI-assisted development needs
    32→the same discipline.
    33→
    34→Sequant provides that discipline.
    35→
    36→---
    37→
    38→## How It Works
    39→
    40→Sequant installs slash commands into Claude Code that enforce a phased
    41→workflow. Each issue moves through plan, build, and review — with an
    42→isolated worktree keeping your main branch clean and a quality loop
    43→that catches problems before merge.
    44→
    45→```text
    46→                         ┌──────────────┐
    47→                         │ GitHub Issue │
    48→                         │    #123      │
    49→                         └──────┬───────┘
    50→                                │
    51→                         ┌──────▼───────┐
    52→                         │    /spec     │  Runs in main repo.
    53→                         │     Plan     │  Reads issue, lints AC,
    54→                         │              │  posts plan to GitHub.
    55→                         └──────┬───────┘
    56→                                │
    57→               ┌────────────────▼──────────────────────┐
    58→               │           WORKTREE                    │
    59→               │    isolated branch + dependencies     │
    60→               │                                       │
    61→               │         ┌──────────────┐              │
    62→               │         │    /exec     │              │
    63→               │         │    Build     │              │
    64→               │         │              │              │
    65→               │         │ Implement,   │              │
    66→               │         │ commit, push,│              │
    67→               │         │ create PR    │              │
    68→               │         └──────┬───────┘              │
    69→               │                │                      │
    70→               │         ┌──────▼───────┐              │
    71→               │         │     /qa      │◄──┐          │
    72→               │         │    Review    │   │          │
    73→               │         │              │   │          │
    74→               │         │ Type safety, │   │ /loop    │
    75→               │         │ security,    │   │ auto-fix │
    76→               │         │ scope, tests │   │ (up to   │
    77→               │         └──────┬───────┘   │  3x)     │
    78→               │                │           │          │
    79→               │           Pass? ───NO──────┘          │
    80→               │                │                      │
    81→               │               YES                     │
    82→               │                │                      │
    83→               └────────────────┼──────────────────────┘
    84→                                │
    85→                         ┌──────▼───────┐
    86→                         │    Merge     │  PR lands on main.
    87→                         │   + Clean    │  Worktree removed.
    88→                         └──────────────┘
    89→                                │
    90→                                ▼
    91→                         Code on main
    92→```
    93→
    94→Or run the full pipeline in one command:
    95→
    96→```text
    97→/fullsolve 123
    98→```
    99→
   100→### Each Phase Is a Fresh Conversation
   101→
   102→This is a deliberate design choice. Phases don't share implicit context.
   103→The QA phase reviews the actual code diff — not a memory of having written
   104→it. The implementation phase reads the spec plan from the issue comment —
   105→not from conversation history.
   106→
   107→Cross-phase context flows through explicit, inspectable channels:
   108→
   109→| Channel | What it carries |
   110→| --- | --- |
   111→| **GitHub Comments** | Spec plans, QA verdicts, and phase markers posted to the issue thread |
   112→| **Git Diff** | What actually changed in the worktree |
   113→| **State File** (`.sequant/`) | Phase progress, AC status, and timestamps tracked locally per issue |
   114→| **Environment** | Issue number, worktree path, and base branch passed between phases |
   115→
   116→This prevents context pollution, keeps phases composable, and makes the
   117→entire workflow auditable by reading issue comments.
   118→
   119→---
   120→
   121→## What Sequant Installs
   122→
   123→Sequant is not a platform or a service. It's a set of files that live
   124→in your project and extend Claude Code's capabilities.
   125→
   126→```text
   127→your-project/
   128→│
   129→├── .claude/
   130→│   ├── skills/              ◄── 18 slash commands (markdown prompts)
   131→│   │   ├── spec/SKILL.md         /spec, /exec, /qa, /fullsolve,
   132→│   │   ├── exec/SKILL.md         /test, /testgen, /loop, /solve,
   133→│   │   ├── qa/SKILL.md           /verify, /docs, /assess, /clean,
   134→│   │   └── ...                   /improve, /reflect, /merger,
   135→│   │                             /security-review, /upstream
   136→│   ├── hooks/               ◄── 2 Claude Code hooks
   137→│   │   ├── pre-tool.sh           Security guardrails, timing
   138→│   │   └── post-tool.sh          Quality observability, formatting
   139→│   └── settings.json        ◄── Hook configuration
   140→│
   141→├── .sequant/
   142→│   ├── state.json           ◄── Workflow state per issue
   143→│   ├── settings.json        ◄── Project configuration
   144→│   ├── metrics.json         ◄── Aggregated run analytics
   145→│   └── logs/                ◄── Structured JSON run logs
   146→│
   147→├── scripts/dev/
   148→│   ├── new-feature.sh       ◄── Create worktree from issue
   149→│   ├── cleanup-worktree.sh  ◄── Remove worktree after merge
   150→│   └── list-worktrees.sh    ◄── Show active worktrees
   151→│
   152→└── .sequant-manifest.json   ◄── Version tracking for updates
   153→```
   154→
   155→Everything is local. No telemetry. No external services. Your code and
   156→workflow data stay in your repository.
   157→
   158→---
   159→
   160→## What the Quality Gates Catch
   161→
   162→The `/qa` phase isn't a rubber stamp. It runs structured checks against
   163→your code and renders one of four verdicts:
   164→
   165→| Verdict | Meaning |
   166→| ------- | ------- |
   167→| **READY_FOR_MERGE** | All acceptance criteria met, quality checks pass |
   168→| **AC_MET_BUT_NOT_A_PLUS** | Functional but has suggestions for improvement |
   169→| **NEEDS_VERIFICATION** | Requires manual testing or confirmation |
   170→| **AC_NOT_MET** | Acceptance criteria not satisfied — needs fixes |
   171→
   172→The checks behind those verdicts:
   173→
   174→```text
   175→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   176→│                     /qa Quality Gates                   │
   177→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
   178→│                                                         │
   179→│  AC Adherence ─── Does the code do what the issue asked │
   180→│                                                         │
   181→│  Type Safety ──── Detects `any`, missing types,         │
   182→│                   unsafe casts                          │
   183→│                                                         │
   184→│  Security ─────── Semgrep static analysis, OWASP        │
   185→│                   checks, secret detection              │
   186→│                                                         │
   187→│  Scope ────────── Flags changes outside issue scope     │
   188→│                                                         │
   189→│  Test Coverage ── File-by-file coverage, critical path  │
   190→│                   flagging, deleted test detection      │
   191→│                                                         │
   192→│  Build ────────── Distinguishes regressions from        │
   193→│                   pre-existing failures                 │
   194→│                                                         │
   195→│  Anti-Patterns ── N+1 queries, empty catch blocks,      │
   196→│                   over-mocking, stale dependencies      │
   197→│                                                         │
   198→└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
   199→```
   200→
   201→When checks fail, `/loop` can automatically parse findings, apply fixes,
   202→and re-run — up to 3 iterations — without human intervention.
   203→
   204→---
   205→
   206→## What Runs Underneath
   207→
   208→The phases are the visible workflow. Underneath, four systems run
   209→continuously to keep everything safe, tracked, and connected:
   210→
   211→```text
   212→  ┌───────────┐  ┌───────────┐  ┌───────────┐  ┌────────────┐
   213→  │  Skills   │  │   Hooks   │  │   State   │  │   GitHub   │
   214→  │           │  │           │  │           │  │  Comments  │
   215→  │ 18 slash  │  │ Pre-tool: │  │ .sequant/ │  │            │
   216→  │ commands  │  │ blocks    │  │ tracks    │  │ Plans,     │
   217→  │ that tell │  │ secrets,  │  │ phase     │  │ verdicts,  │
   218→  │ Claude    │  │ unsafe    │  │ progress, │  │ and phase  │
   219→  │ Code what │  │ commands  │  │ metrics,  │  │ markers    │
   220→  │ to do in  │  │           │  │ run logs  │  │ that carry │
   221→  │ each      │  │ Post-tool:│  │           │  │ context    │
   222→  │ phase     │  │ formats,  │  │ Updated   │  │ between    │
   223→  │           │  │ observes  │  │ at phase  │  │ sessions   │
   224→  │           │  │ quality   │  │ boundaries│  │            │
   225→  └───────────┘  └───────────┘  └───────────┘  └────────────┘
   226→```
   227→
   228→- **Skills** are markdown prompts — they tell Claude Code exactly what to
   229→  do in each phase. The intelligence lives in the prompts.
   230→- **Hooks** fire on every tool call. Pre-tool hooks block secrets,
   231→  destructive commands, and edits outside the worktree. Post-tool hooks
   232→  auto-format code and log quality signals.
   233→- **State** tracks phase progress locally in `.sequant/state.json`,
   234→  aggregates metrics across runs, and writes structured JSON logs.
   235→- **GitHub Comments** carry context between phases. The spec plan, QA
   236→  verdict, and phase markers are all posted to the issue thread —
   237→  making the workflow resumable across sessions and machines.
   238→
   239→---
   240→
   241→## Worktree Isolation
   242→
   243→The worktree in the diagram above isn't just a feature — it's the
   244→boundary between planning and execution. `/spec` runs in your main
   245→repo (no code changes). Everything inside the worktree — `/exec`,
   246→`/qa`, `/loop` — operates on an isolated branch with its own
   247→dependencies. Your main branch is never touched.
   248→
   249→```text
   250→your-project/                     ◄── Main repo (stays on main)
   251→
   252→../worktrees/feature/
   253→    ├── 123-add-auth/             ◄── Issue #123 (own branch + deps)
   254→    ├── 124-fix-nav-bug/          ◄── Issue #124 (own branch + deps)
   255→    └── 125-update-api/           ◄── Issue #125 (own branch + deps)
   256→```
   257→
   258→Work on multiple issues in parallel. Discard failed experiments. When
   259→a PR merges, the worktree is cleaned up and the code lands on main.
   260→
   261→---
   262→
   263→## Two Ways to Use It
   264→
   265→### Interactive — Inside Claude Code
   266→
   267→Type slash commands directly. Review plans before building. Step through
   268→at your own pace.
   269→
   270→```text
   271→/spec 123          Plan the implementation
   272→/exec 123          Build it
   273→/qa 123            Review it
   274→```
   275→
   276→### Headless — From the CLI
   277→
   278→Run the full pipeline without the Claude Code UI. Batch multiple issues.
   279→Let it iterate overnight.
   280→
   281→```bash
   282→npx sequant run 123                    # Single issue, full pipeline
   283→npx sequant run 1 2 3                  # Three issues in parallel
   284→npx sequant run 123 --quality-loop     # Auto-fix on QA failure
   285→npx sequant run 123 --resume           # Pick up where you left off
   286→```
   287→
   288→---
   289→
   290→## Issues as Building Blocks
   291→
   292→The core idea behind Sequant is that a project is a collection of
   293→well-defined issues, and each issue follows the same lifecycle:
   294→
   295→```text
   296→              ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
   297→              │              Your Project                 │
   298→              │                                           │
   299→              │   ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐         │
   300→              │   │ #101  │  │ #102  │  │ #103  │         │
   301→              │   │ Auth  │  │ Nav   │  │ API   │  ...    │
   302→              │   │ [done]│  │ [done]│  │ [wip] │         │
   303→              │   └───────┘  └───────┘  └───────┘         │
   304→              │                                           │
   305→              │   ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐         │
   306→              │   │ #104  │  │ #105  │  │ #106  │         │
   307→              │   │ Tests │  │ Perf  │  │ Docs  │  ...    │
   308→              │   │ [plan]│  │ [plan]│  │ [plan]│         │
   309→              │   └───────┘  └───────┘  └───────┘         │
   310→              │                                           │
   311→              └───────────────────────────────────────────┘
   312→```
   313→
   314→Each block passes through the same phases. Each block gets the same
   315→quality checks. The output is predictable because the process is
   316→consistent — not because every issue is simple, but because every
   317→issue is treated with the same rigor.
   318→
   319→Sequant doesn't make Claude Code less flexible. It makes the workflow
   320→around it more structured. You still get the full power of Claude Code
   321→inside each phase. Sequant just ensures that power is applied
   322→methodically — plan first, build in isolation, review before merging.
   323→
   324→---
   325→
   326→## Quick Start
   327→
   328→```bash
   329→npm install sequant
   330→npx sequant init
   331→npx sequant doctor
   332→```
   333→
   334→Then in Claude Code:
   335→
   336→```text
   337→/fullsolve 123
   338→```
   339→
   340→See the [Quickstart Guide](/docs/getting-started/quickstart) for the full walkthrough.

1→# What We've Built: Sequant v1.20.3
     2→
     3→> **Quantize your development workflow** — Sequential AI phases with quality gates
     4→
     5→Sequant transforms the chaos of AI-assisted development into
     6→a structured, repeatable process. Every GitHub issue becomes a
     7→journey through planning, implementation, testing, and review
     8→— with quality gates at every step.
     9→

    30→## The Big Picture
    31→
    32→```
    33→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    34→│                          GitHub Issue #123                          │
    35→│                    "Add user authentication"                        │
    36→└───────────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘
    37→                                │
    38→                                ▼
    39→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    40→│                           Claude Code                               │
    41→│                                                                     │
    42→│   /spec ──────► /exec ──────► /test ──────► /qa ──────► PR Ready    │
    43→│   (Plan)       (Build)      (Verify)      (Review)                  │
    44→│                                                                     │
    45→│   ─────────────── or just: /fullsolve 123 ──────────────            │
    46→└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    47→                                │
    48→                    ┌───────────┼───────────┐
    49→                    │           │           │
    50→            ┌───────▼───────┐   │   ┌───────▼───────┐
    51→            │ Git Worktrees │   │   │   Quality     │
    52→            │  (isolation)  │   │   │    Checks     │
    53→            └───────────────┘   │   └───────────────┘
    54→                        ┌───────▼───────┐
    55→                        │    GitHub     │
    56→                        │   CLI (gh)    │
    57→                        └───────────────┘
    58→```
    59→
    60→### Why Sequant Exists
    61→
    62→AI coding assistants are powerful but chaotic. Without structure, you get:
    63→- Half-finished features scattered across branches
    64→- Acceptance criteria ignored or misunderstood
    65→- No clear definition of "done"
    66→- Security issues slipping through
    67→
    68→Sequant brings **discipline without friction**:
    69→- **Phases** ensure nothing gets skipped
    70→- **Worktrees** keep each issue isolated
    71→- **Quality gates** catch problems before merge
    72→- **Local state** tracks what's done and what's not
    73→
    74→### Phase Isolation by Design
    75→
    76→Each phase runs as a **fresh conversation** — no implicit memory carried forward. This is intentional:
    77→
    78→- **No context pollution** — stale planning notes don't crowd out implementation context
    79→- **Honest review** — `/qa` evaluates the actual diff, not the implementer's memory of it
    80→- **Composable phases** — run `/qa` alone, re-run `/exec` after failure, skip phases freely
    81→
    82→Cross-phase context flows through **explicit channels**:
    83→`state.json` (phase progress, AC status), GitHub issue
    84→comments (spec plans, QA verdicts), git diff (what actually
    85→changed), and environment variables (orchestration context).
    86→These are inspectable, scoped per issue, and don't accumulate
    87→noise.
    88→
    89→---
    90→
    91→## Skill Commands
    92→
    93→Sequant provides **18 slash commands** organized by purpose. Run them inside Claude Code.
    94→
    95→### Core Workflow
    96→
    97→The four pillars of the Sequant workflow:
    98→
    99→| Command | Purpose | What It Does |
   100→|---------|---------|--------------|
   101→| `/spec` | **Plan** | Extracts AC, creates implementation plans, posts to GitHub |
   102→| `/exec` | **Build** | Creates worktrees, implements incrementally, creates PRs |
   103→| `/test` | **Verify** | Browser automation via Chrome DevTools, screenshot evidence |
   104→| `/qa` | **Review** | Validates AC, runs quality checks, renders merge verdicts |
   105→
   106→**`/spec` details:**
   107→- Lints AC for vague/unmeasurable terms
   108→- Requires explicit verification methods (Unit/Integration/Browser/Manual)
   109→- Feature Quality Planning (6 dimensions: completeness, error handling,
   110→  code quality, test coverage, best practices, polish)
   111→- Scope Assessment with verdicts: `SCOPE_OK` / `SCOPE_WARNING` /
   112→  `SCOPE_SPLIT_RECOMMENDED`
   113→- Content analysis (title/body patterns → phase recommendations)
   114→- Testgen auto-detection, conflict detection, Derived ACs
   115→
   116→**`/exec` details:**
   117→- Pre-PR AC verification (checks each AC before PR creation)
   118→- Shell script quality checks
   119→- Mandatory prompt templates for parallel sub-agents
   120→
   121→**`/test` details:**
   122→- Coverage analysis (warns when changed files lack tests)
   123→- Graceful fallback to manual checklists
   124→
   125→**`/qa` details:**
   126→- Type safety, security scans, Semgrep static analysis
   127→- Test tautology detection (flags tests that don't call production code)
   128→- Call-site review (verifies new functions are called correctly, not just implemented correctly)
   129→- CI status awareness, build verification against main
   130→- Caches expensive checks (`--no-cache` to force fresh)
   131→- Verdicts: `READY_FOR_MERGE`, `AC_NOT_MET`,
   132→  `NEEDS_VERIFICATION`, `SECURITY_CONCERN`
   133→
   134→### Automation Commands
   135→
   136→For when you want to go hands-off:
   137→
   138→| Command | Purpose | What It Does |
   139→|---------|---------|--------------|
   140→| `/fullsolve` | **End-to-End** | Orchestrates spec→exec→test→qa with auto-fix loops |
   141→| `/solve` | **Advisor** | Recommends optimal workflow, outputs CLI commands |
   142→| `/loop` | **Quality Loop** | Parses findings, applies fixes, re-validates |
   143→
   144→### Testing & Verification
   145→
   146→| Command | Purpose | What It Does |
   147→|---------|---------|--------------|
   148→| `/testgen` | **Test Generator** | Creates stubs from `/spec` verification criteria |
   149→| `/verify` | **CLI Verification** | Runs commands, captures output, posts evidence |
   150→| `/docs` | **Documentation** | Auto-detects doc type, generates operational docs |
   151→
   152→### Analysis & Utilities
   153→
   154→| Command | Purpose | What It Does |
   155→|---------|---------|--------------|
   156→| `/assess` | **Triage** | Detects current phase, recommends next action |
   157→| `/clean` | **Cleanup** | Archives stale files, verifies build, commits |
   158→| `/improve` | **Discovery** | Scans for issues, creates GitHub issues |
   159→| `/reflect` | **Learning** | Analyzes session effectiveness |
   160→| `/security-review` | **Security** | Domain-specific checklists, threat modeling |
   161→| `/merger` | **Integration** | Multi-issue merge with conflict detection |
   162→| `/upstream` | **Tracking** | Monitors Claude Code releases, creates issues |
   163→
   164→### Shared Resources
   165→
   166→Skills share common references in `skills/_shared/`:
   167→- `subagent-types.md` — Valid sub-agent types
   168→- `prompt-templates.md` — Task-specific sub-agent templates
   169→

   171→
   172→## CLI Commands
   173→
   174→The `sequant` CLI provides **10 commands** for project management.
   175→
   176→```bash
   177→npm install -g sequant   # Install globally
   178→npx sequant              # Or run via npx
   179→```
   180→
   181→| Command | Description |
   182→|---------|-------------|
   183→| `sequant init` | Initialize Sequant in a project (copies templates, creates `.claude/` and `.sequant/`) |
   184→| `sequant doctor` | Check installation health — prerequisites, closed-issue verification, config validation |
   185→| `sequant run <issues>` | Execute workflow (`-q` quality loop, `--chain`, `--resume`, `--force`) |
   186→| `sequant status` | Show version, config, tracked issues with cleanup options |
   187→| `sequant update` | Update skill templates to latest versions |
   188→| `sequant state` | Manage workflow state (`init`, `rebuild`, `clean`) |
   189→| `sequant stats` | View local workflow analytics — success rates, timing, phase distribution |
   190→| `sequant logs` | View and manage log files with rotation |
   191→| `sequant merge` | Batch-level integration QA — verify feature branches before merging (`--check`, `--scan`, `--post`) |
   192→| `sequant dashboard` | Launch real-time workflow dashboard (Hono-powered) |

1→# Contributing to Sequant
     2→
     3→Thank you for your interest in contributing to Sequant! This document provides guidelines and information for contributors.
     4→
     5→## Getting Started
     6→
     7→### Prerequisites
     8→
     9→- Node.js 18.0.0 or higher
    10→- Git
    11→- GitHub CLI (`gh`) for testing issue integration
    12→
    13→### Setup
    14→
    15→```bash
    16→# Clone the repository
    17→git clone https://github.com/sequant-io/sequant.git
    18→cd sequant
    19→
    20→# Install dependencies
    21→npm install
    22→
    23→# Build the project
    24→npm run build
    25→
    26→# Run in development mode
    27→npm run dev -- --help
    28→```
    29→
    30→## Development Workflow
    31→
    32→### Running Locally
    33→
    34→```bash
    35→# Run CLI commands in dev mode
    36→npm run dev -- init --help
    37→npm run dev -- doctor
    38→npm run dev -- run 1 --dry-run
    39→
    40→# Build and test
    41→npm run build
    42→npm run lint
    43→npm run validate:skills
    44→```
    45→
    46→### Project Structure
    47→
    48→```
    49→sequant/
    50→├── bin/cli.ts              # CLI entry point
    51→├── src/
    52→│   ├── commands/           # CLI command implementations
    53→│   │   ├── init.ts
    54→│   │   ├── update.ts
    55→│   │   ├── doctor.ts
    56→│   │   ├── status.ts
    57→│   │   └── run.ts
    58→│   ├── lib/                # Shared utilities
    59→│   │   ├── fs.ts           # File system helpers
    60→│   │   ├── manifest.ts     # Manifest management
    61→│   │   ├── stacks.ts       # Stack detection
    62→│   │   ├── system.ts       # System checks (gh, jq)
    63→│   │   ├── templates.ts    # Template processing
    64→│   │   └── workflow/       # Workflow execution
    65→│   └── index.ts            # Public exports
    66→├── templates/              # Skill and hook templates
    67→│   ├── skills/             # 14 workflow skills
    68→│   ├── hooks/              # Pre/post tool hooks
    69→│   ├── memory/             # Constitution template
    70→│   └── scripts/            # Shell script helpers
    71→├── stacks/                 # Stack configuration files
    72→│   ├── nextjs.yaml
    73→│   ├── rust.yaml
    74→│   ├── python.yaml
    75→│   └── go.yaml
    76→└── docs/                   # Documentation
    77→```
    78→
    79→### Script Locations
    80→
    81→Helper scripts exist in two locations:
    82→
    83→| Location | Purpose | Tracked |
    84→|----------|---------|---------|
    85→| `templates/scripts/` | Canonical source scripts | ✅ Yes |
    86→| `scripts/dev/` | Symlinks to templates (created by `sequant init`) | ❌ No (gitignored) |
    87→
    88→**When modifying scripts:** Always change files in `templates/scripts/` — this is what gets committed and distributed to users.
    89→
    90→The `scripts/dev/` directory contains symlinks pointing to `templates/scripts/`, created automatically by `sequant init`. This means template updates are immediately available without re-running init.
    91→
    92→> ⚠️ **Warning:** Do not edit files in `scripts/dev/` directly — they are symlinks to the source templates. Any local changes should be made in `templates/scripts/`. On Windows without symlink support, `scripts/dev/` contains copies that will be overwritten on the next `sequant init`.
    93→
    94→## Making Changes
    95→
    96→### Adding a New Command
    97→
    98→1. Create a new file in `src/commands/`:
    99→
   100→```typescript
   101→// src/commands/mycommand.ts
   102→import chalk from "chalk";
   103→
   104→interface MyCommandOptions {
   105→  flag?: boolean;
   106→}
   107→
   108→export async function myCommand(options: MyCommandOptions): Promise<void> {
   109→  console.log(chalk.blue("Running my command..."));
   110→  // Implementation
   111→}
   112→```
   113→
   114→2. Register in `bin/cli.ts`:
   115→
   116→```typescript
   117→import { myCommand } from "../src/commands/mycommand.js";
   118→
   119→program
   120→  .command("mycommand")
   121→  .description("Description of my command")
   122→  .option("-f, --flag", "Option description")
   123→  .action(myCommand);
   124→```
   125→
   126→### Adding a New Skill
   127→
   128→1. Create directory: `templates/skills/myskill/`
   129→2. Create `SKILL.md` with YAML frontmatter:
   130→
   131→```markdown
   132→---
   133→name: myskill
   134→description: "What this skill does"
   135→license: MIT
   136→metadata:
   137→  author: your-name
   138→  version: "1.0"
   139→allowed-tools:
   140→  - Read
   141→  - Edit
   142→  - Bash(npm test:*)
   143→---
   144→
   145→# My Skill
   146→
   147→Instructions for the AI agent...
   148→```
   149→
   150→3. Validate with: `npx skills-ref validate templates/skills/myskill`
   151→
   152→### Adding a New Stack
   153→
   154→1. Create `stacks/mystack.yaml`:
   155→
   156→```yaml
   157→name: mystack
   158→displayName: My Stack
   159→description: Description of the stack
   160→
   161→detection:
   162→  files:
   163→    - mystack.config.js
   164→  packageDeps:
   165→    - mystack-core
   166→
   167→commands:
   168→  test: mystack test
   169→  build: mystack build
   170→  lint: mystack lint
   171→
   172→variables:
   173→  TEST_COMMAND: mystack test
   174→  BUILD_COMMAND: mystack build
   175→  LINT_COMMAND: mystack lint
   176→
   177→patterns:
   178→  src: src/**/*.ms
   179→  tests: tests/**/*.ms
   180→```
   181→
   182→2. Update `src/lib/stacks.ts` if needed for special handling.
   183→
   184→## Code Style
   185→
   186→- TypeScript strict mode enabled
   187→- ESLint for linting (`npm run lint`)
   188→- Use `chalk` for colored output
   189→- Use async/await for asynchronous operations
   190→- Export types from `src/index.ts` for public API
   191→
   192→### Commit Messages
   193→
   194→Follow conventional commits:
   195→
   196→```
   197→feat: Add new feature
   198→fix: Fix a bug
   199→docs: Documentation changes
   200→ci: CI/CD changes
   201→refactor: Code refactoring
   202→test: Add or update tests
   203→chore: Maintenance tasks
   204→```
   205→
   206→## Testing
   207→
   208→### Manual Testing
   209→
   210→```bash
   211→# Test initialization
   212→npm run dev -- init --stack nextjs --yes
   213→
   214→# Test doctor
   215→npm run dev -- doctor
   216→
   217→# Test run command
   218→npm run dev -- run 1 --dry-run --verbose
   219→```
   220→
   221→### Skills Validation
   222→
   223→```bash
   224→# Validate all skills
   225→npm run validate:skills
   226→
   227→# Validate single skill
   228→npx skills-ref validate templates/skills/spec
   229→```
   230→
   231→### CI Checks
   232→
   233→Before submitting a PR, ensure:
   234→
   235→```bash
   236→npm run lint        # No lint errors
   237→npm run build       # Build succeeds
   238→npm run validate:skills  # All skills valid
   239→```
   240→
   241→## Publishing to npm (Maintainers)
   242→
   243→### Setup
   244→
   245→npm requires 2FA for publishing. You have two options:
   246→
   247→**Option 1: OTP Code (Interactive)**
   248→```bash
   249→npm publish --otp=YOUR_CODE
   250→```
   251→
   252→**Option 2: Automation Token (CI/Scripts)**
   253→
   254→1. Go to https://www.npmjs.com/settings/~/tokens/granular-access-tokens/new
   255→2. Create token with:
   256→   - **Packages**: Select `sequant`
   257→   - **Permissions**: Read and Write
   258→   - **Bypass 2FA**: ✅ Enabled (required for automation)
   259→3. Configure:
   260→   ```bash
   261→   npm config set //registry.npmjs.org/:_authToken=npm_YOUR_TOKEN
   262→   ```
   263→
   264→### Release Process
   265→
   266→**Recommended: Use the `/release` skill** (requires Claude Code):
   267→
   268→```bash
   269→# Dry-run to preview
   270→/release --dry-run
   271→
   272→# Execute release
   273→/release patch   # Bug fixes (1.3.1 → 1.3.2)
   274→/release minor   # New features (1.3.1 → 1.4.0)
   275→/release major   # Breaking changes (1.3.1 → 2.0.0)
   276→```
   277→
   278→The `/release` skill automates all steps below with pre-flight checks.
   279→
   280→<details>
   281→<summary>Manual Release Process</summary>
   282→
   283→```bash
   284→# 1. Update version and changelog
   285→npm version 1.x.x --no-git-tag-version
   286→# Edit CHANGELOG.md
   287→
   288→# 2. Commit and tag
   289→git add -A
   290→git commit -m "chore: release v1.x.x"
   291→git tag -a v1.x.x -m "Release v1.x.x"
   292→
   293→# 3. Push
   294→git push origin main
   295→git push origin v1.x.x
   296→
   297→# 4. Create GitHub release
   298→gh release create v1.x.x --title "v1.x.x" --notes "Release notes..."
   299→
   300→# 5. Publish to npm
   301→npm publish
   302→```
   303→
   304→</details>
   305→
   306→## Pull Requests
   307→
   308→1. Fork the repository
   309→2. Create a feature branch: `git checkout -b feature/my-feature`
   310→3. Make your changes
   311→4. Run tests and linting
   312→5. Commit with a descriptive message
   313→6. Push and create a PR
   314→
   315→### PR Checklist
   316→
   317→- [ ] Code follows project style
   318→- [ ] Changes are documented
   319→- [ ] All CI checks pass
   320→- [ ] Skills validated (if modified)
   321→- [ ] README updated (if needed)
   322→
   323→## Reporting Issues
   324→
   325→When reporting bugs, include:
   326→
   327→- Sequant version (`sequant --version`)
   328→- Node.js version (`node --version`)
   329→- Operating system
   330→- Steps to reproduce
   331→- Expected vs actual behavior
   332→- Error messages or logs
   333→
   334→**For plugin-specific issues**, use the [Plugin Feedback template](https://github.com/sequant-io/sequant/issues/new?template=plugin-feedback.yml) which collects additional context like installation method, affected skills, and MCP configuration.
   335→
   336→## Plugin-Specific Contributions
   337→
   338→If you installed Sequant as a Claude Code plugin, here's how to contribute:
   339→
   340→### Testing Plugin Changes Locally
   341→
   342→```bash
   343→# Clone the repo to test changes
   344→git clone https://github.com/sequant-io/sequant.git
   345→cd sequant
   346→
   347→# The plugin skills are in the skills/ directory (not templates/)
   348→# These are the files that get loaded when using the plugin
   349→
   350→# Test skill changes by running Claude Code in this directory
   351→# Skills in skills/ will override your installed plugin
   352→```
   353→
   354→### Plugin Directory Structure
   355→
   356→When contributing to the plugin, understand these key directories:
   357→
   358→| Directory | Purpose | Used By |
   359→|-----------|---------|---------|
   360→| `skills/` | Plugin skills (loaded by Claude Code plugin system) | Plugin users |
   361→| `templates/skills/` | Template skills (copied by `sequant init`) | npm users |
   362→| `hooks/` | Plugin hooks (pre-tool, post-tool) | Plugin users |
   363→| `templates/hooks/` | Template hooks (copied by `sequant init`) | npm users |
   364→| `memory/` | Plugin memory (constitution) | Plugin users |
   365→| `scripts/` | Plugin helper scripts | Plugin users |
   366→
   367→**Important:** If you modify a skill, update BOTH locations:
   368→- `skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md` - For plugin users
   369→- `templates/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md` - For npm users
   370→
   371→### Contributing New Skills
   372→
   373→1. Create skill in both locations:
   374→   ```bash
   375→   mkdir -p skills/myskill templates/skills/myskill
   376→   ```
   377→
   378→2. Create `SKILL.md` following the [skill format](#adding-a-new-skill)
   379→
   380→3. Update `skills/_shared/references/` if adding shared references
   381→
   382→4. Test with the plugin:
   383→   ```bash
   384→   # In a test project, temporarily point to your local clone
   385→   # by setting up a local marketplace or using direct path
   386→   ```
   387→
   388→5. Validate both locations:
   389→   ```bash
   390→   npx skills-ref validate skills/myskill
   391→   npx skills-ref validate templates/skills/myskill
   392→   ```
   393→
   394→### Plugin-Specific Testing
   395→
   396→When testing plugin changes:
   397→
   398→1. **Hook testing:** Check `/tmp/claude-hooks.log` for hook execution logs
   399→2. **Skill testing:** Use `/assess <issue>` to verify skill availability
   400→3. **MCP fallbacks:** Test with MCPs disabled to ensure graceful degradation
   401→
   402→### Reporting Plugin Issues
   403→
   404→Use `/improve` within Claude Code to analyze issues, or file directly:
   405→
   406→- **Plugin bugs:** [Plugin Feedback template](https://github.com/sequant-io/sequant/issues/new?template=plugin-feedback.yml)
   407→- **Skill issues:** Include which skill and the error message
   408→- **Hook issues:** Include contents of `/tmp/claude-hooks.log`
   409→
   410→## Questions?
   411→
   412→- Open a [GitHub Issue](https://github.com/sequant-io/sequant/issues)
   413→- Check existing issues for similar questions
   414→- Use [GitHub Discussions](https://github.com/sequant-io/sequant/discussions) for general questions
   415→
   416→## Telemetry
   417→
   418→Sequant does not collect any usage telemetry. See [docs/reference/telemetry.md](docs/reference/telemetry.md) for our reasoning.
   419→
   420→## License
   421→
   422→By contributing, you agree that your contributions will be licensed under the MIT License.
   423→

1→---
     2→title: "Complete Workflow Guide"
     3→description: "This guide covers the full Sequant workflow, including post-QA patterns used by experienced users."
     4→draft: false
     5→---
     6→
     7→This guide covers the full Sequant workflow, including post-QA patterns used by experienced users.
     8→
     9→## Workflow Overview
    10→
    11→```
    12→┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐
    13→│  /spec  │───▶│  /exec  │───▶│  /test  │───▶│   /qa   │───▶│  /docs  │───▶│  Merge  │
    14→└─────────┘    └─────────┘    └─────────┘    └─────────┘    └─────────┘    └─────────┘
    15→     │              │              │              │              │              │
    16→     ▼              ▼              ▼              ▼              ▼              ▼
    17→   Plan          Build       Verify (UI)     Review      Document      Ship
    18→                                                │
    19→                                                ▼
    20→                                         Gap Analysis
    21→                                         (see below)
    22→```
    23→
    24→## Phase 1: Spec
    25→
    26→```
    27→/spec 123
    28→```
    29→
    30→**What happens:**
    31→- Reads issue description and comments
    32→- Analyzes codebase for patterns
    33→- Drafts acceptance criteria (ACs)
    34→- Creates implementation plan
    35→- Posts plan as issue comment
    36→
    37→**Output:** GitHub issue comment with AC checklist and implementation plan.
    38→
    39→**When to skip:** Simple bug fixes, typos, documentation-only changes.
    40→
    41→## Phase 2: Exec
    42→
    43→```
    44→/exec 123
    45→```
    46→
    47→**What happens:**
    48→- Creates isolated git worktree (`feature/123-issue-title`)
    49→- Implements changes per the spec
    50→- Runs tests after changes
    51→- Creates commits with progress
    52→- Creates PR
    53→
    54→**Output:** Feature branch with implementation, open PR.
    55→
    56→## Phase 3: Test/Verify (Optional)
    57→
    58→```
    59→/test 123    # Browser-based UI testing (requires Chrome DevTools MCP)
    60→/verify 123  # CLI/script execution verification
    61→```
    62→
    63→**When to use:**
    64→- `/test` for UI changes
    65→- `/verify` for CLI tools, scripts, commands
    66→
    67→## Phase 4: QA
    68→
    69→```
    70→/qa 123
    71→```
    72→
    73→**What happens:**
    74→- Reviews code against all ACs (including derived ACs from spec)
    75→- Checks type safety, security, scope
    76→- Verifies CI status
    77→- Generates detailed report
    78→
    79→**Verdicts:**
    80→| Verdict | Meaning | Action |
    81→|---------|---------|--------|
    82→| `READY_FOR_MERGE` | All ACs met, high quality | Merge |
    83→| `AC_MET_BUT_NOT_A_PLUS` | ACs met, minor improvements suggested | Can merge |
    84→| `NEEDS_VERIFICATION` | Waiting on CI or external check | Wait, re-run |
    85→| `AC_NOT_MET` | ACs not fully met | Fix issues |
    86→
    87→If QA finds issues, run `/loop 123` to auto-fix (up to 3 iterations).
    88→
    89→## Phase 5: Gap Analysis
    90→
    91→**This is the key step most users miss.**
    92→
    93→After QA passes, ask: **"Any other gaps?"**
    94→
    95→This catches:
    96→- Cross-platform compatibility issues
    97→- Design concerns for future maintainability
    98→- Edge cases not covered by ACs
    99→- Documentation completeness
   100→
   101→### Gap Handling
   102→
   103→| Gap Type | Action |
   104→|----------|--------|
   105→| Quick fix (< 5 min) | Fix inline, commit, push |
   106→| Design consideration | Create follow-up issue |
   107→| Out of scope | Create separate issue |
   108→| Acceptable debt | Document and proceed |
   109→
   110→### Example Gap Analysis
   111→
   112→```
   113→User: any other gaps?
   114→
   115→Claude: Looking critically...
   116→
   117→1. **Cross-platform sed compatibility** (minor)
   118→   - The extraction pattern works on GNU sed but not macOS BSD sed
   119→   - Resolution: Fixed - replaced with portable `grep -oE`
   120→
   121→2. **Hardcoded dimension names** (medium)
   122→   - Maintenance burden if new dimensions are added
   123→   - Resolution: Created issue #227 for future improvement
   124→```
   125→
   126→## Phase 6: Docs
   127→
   128→```
   129→/docs 123
   130→```
   131→
   132→**What happens:**
   133→- Checks if CHANGELOG needs updating
   134→- Updates relevant documentation files
   135→- Ensures user-facing changes are documented
   136→
   137→**Files typically updated:**
   138→- `CHANGELOG.md` - For user-visible changes
   139→- `docs/internal/what-weve-built.md` - For feature tracking
   140→- `README.md` - If public API changes
   141→- Feature-specific docs in `docs/`
   142→
   143→**When to skip:** Internal refactors, test-only changes.
   144→
   145→## Phase 7: Merge
   146→
   147→```
   148→gh pr merge --squash
   149→```
   150→
   151→For multi-issue batches, run merge checks first, then merge:
   152→
   153→```bash
   154→sequant merge --check         # Verify cross-issue integration
   155→/merger 123 124 125           # Merge all issues
   156→```
   157→
   158→`sequant merge --check` catches merge conflicts, template mirroring gaps, and file overlaps at zero AI cost. See [Merge Command Reference](/docs/reference/merge-command).
   159→
   160→## Phase 8: Smoke Test
   161→
   162→After merge, verify the feature works on main:
   163→
   164→```bash
   165→git checkout main && git pull
   166→npm run build        # Verify build
   167→npm test             # Verify tests
   168→# Manual verification of the specific feature
   169→```
   170→
   171→### Smoke Test Checklist
   172→
   173→- [ ] Build passes on main
   174→- [ ] Tests pass on main
   175→- [ ] Feature works as expected
   176→- [ ] No regressions in related areas
   177→
   178→## Complete Example Session
   179→
   180→```
   181→/fullsolve 223                    # Run complete pipeline
   182→
   183→# After fullsolve completes...
   184→/qa 223                           # Second QA pass
   185→
   186→User: any other gaps?
   187→Claude: [identifies 2 gaps]
   188→
   189→User: fill all gaps or create new issues if too complex
   190→Claude: [fixes minor gap, creates issue for complex one]
   191→
   192→User: do you need to update docs?
   193→/docs 223                         # Update CHANGELOG, what-weve-built
   194→
   195→User: merge then smoketest
   196→Claude: [merges PR, runs smoke tests]
   197→```
   198→
   199→## Choosing Your Workflow
   200→
   201→### Use `/fullsolve` when:
   202→- Standard features or bug fixes
   203→- ACs are clear from the issue
   204→- You want minimal intervention
   205→
   206→### Use step-by-step when:
   207→- Complex features needing human review at each phase
   208→- Unclear requirements needing iteration
   209→- Learning the workflow
   210→
   211→### Use `sequant run` when:
   212→- Batch processing multiple issues
   213→- Headless/CI execution
   214→- Parallel processing
   215→
   216→```bash
   217→npx sequant run 1 2 3            # Multiple issues in parallel
   218→npx sequant run 123 --quality-loop   # Auto-fix until QA passes
   219→```
   220→
   221→## Common Patterns
   222→
   223→### Pattern: Simple Bug Fix
   224→
   225→```
   226→/exec 123    # Skip spec for simple fixes
   227→/qa 123
   228→gh pr merge --squash
   229→```
   230→
   231→### Pattern: UI Feature
   232→
   233→```
   234→/spec 123
   235→/exec 123
   236→/test 123    # Browser-based verification
   237→/qa 123
   238→/docs 123
   239→gh pr merge --squash
   240→```
   241→
   242→### Pattern: Multi-Issue Integration
   243→
   244→```
   245→/fullsolve 123
   246→/fullsolve 124
   247→/fullsolve 125
   248→sequant merge --check         # Verify cross-issue integration
   249→/merger 123 124 125           # Merge all issues
   250→```
   251→
   252→### Pattern: Quality Iteration
   253→
   254→```
   255→/spec 123
   256→/exec 123
   257→/qa 123              # Returns AC_NOT_MET
   258→/loop 123            # Auto-fix
   259→/qa 123              # Re-verify
   260→```
   261→
   262→## Tips
   263→
   264→### Be Thorough with Gap Analysis
   265→
   266→The "any other gaps?" step catches issues that automated QA misses:
   267→- Platform-specific behavior
   268→- Maintainability concerns
   269→- Documentation completeness
   270→
   271→### Create Issues for Complex Gaps
   272→
   273→Don't try to fix everything inline. If a gap needs design consideration:
   274→
   275→```
   276→User: fill gaps or create new issues if too complex
   277→Claude: Created issue #227 for [complex gap description]
   278→```
   279→
   280→### Always Smoke Test
   281→
   282→Even with passing CI, verify the feature works on main after merge. This catches integration issues.
   283→
   284→### Track Derived ACs
   285→
   286→Spec generates "derived ACs" from quality planning. These flow through exec and qa:
   287→
   288→```
   289→Original ACs: AC-1 through AC-5 (from issue)
   290→Derived ACs: AC-6, AC-7 (from spec quality planning)
   291→```
   292→
   293→Both are tracked equally in QA verdicts.
   294→
   295→## Troubleshooting
   296→
   297→### QA keeps finding issues
   298→
   299→Run `/loop 123` for automatic fixes, or manually address based on QA feedback.
   300→
   301→### CI is pending
   302→
   303→Wait for CI, then re-run `/qa 123`. The verdict will update based on CI status.
   304→
   305→### Build fails but it's not my fault
   306→
   307→QA verifies if build failure is a regression or pre-existing on main. Pre-existing failures don't block merge.
   308→
   309→### Worktree is in bad state
   310→
   311→```bash
   312→# Remove and recreate
   313→git worktree remove ../worktrees/feature/123-*
   314→/exec 123  # Creates fresh worktree
   315→```
   316→
   317→## Next Steps
   318→
   319→- [Quality Gates](/docs/concepts/quality-gates) - What QA checks for
   320→- [Git Workflows](/docs/guides/git-workflows) - Worktree management
   321→- [Customization](/docs/guides/customization) - Configure Sequant behavior
   322→

    71→
    72→## Why Sequant?
    73→
    74→When using AI coding assistants, work can become scattered and quality inconsistent. Sequant solves this by:
    75→
    76→- **Consistent quality** — Every issue goes through the same review gates
    77→- **Traceable decisions** — Plans and progress documented in GitHub issues
    78→- **Isolated work** — Git worktrees prevent half-finished features from polluting main
    79→- **AI-assisted** — Claude Code handles implementation while you review and approve
    80→
    81→---
    82→
    83→## Get Started
    84→
    85→```bash
    86→# Install and initialize
    87→npx sequant init
    88→
    89→# Verify setup
    90→npx sequant doctor
    91→
    92→# Start with any issue
    93→/spec 123
    94→```
    95→
    96→See [Your First Workflow](getting-started/first-workflow.md) for a complete walkthrough.
    97→

1→---
     2→title: "Run Command"
     3→description: "Quick Start: Execute the Sequant workflow (`/spec` → `/exec` → `/qa`) for one or more GitHub issues with a single command. Use this to automate issue resolution through sequential AI phases."
     4→draft: false
     5→---
     6→
     7→**Quick Start:** Execute the Sequant workflow (`/spec` → `/exec` → `/qa`) for one or more GitHub issues with a single command. Use this to automate issue resolution through sequential AI phases.
     8→
     9→## Access
    10→
    11→- **Command:** `npx sequant run <issues...> [options]`
    12→- **Requirements:**
    13→  - Sequant initialized (`sequant init`)
    14→  - Claude CLI installed and configured
    15→  - GitHub CLI authenticated (`gh auth login`)
    16→
    17→## Usage
    18→
    19→### Running a Single Issue
    20→
    21→```bash
    22→npx sequant run 123
    23→```
    24→
    25→Executes the default workflow phases (spec → exec → qa) for issue #123.
    26→
    27→### Running Multiple Issues
    28→
    29→```bash
    30→npx sequant run 100 101 102
    31→```
    32→
    33→Processes issues #100, #101, and #102. By default, issues continue processing even if one fails.
    34→
    35→### Sequential Mode with Dependencies
    36→
    37→```bash
    38→npx sequant run 100 101 102 --sequential
    39→```
    40→
    41→Processes issues in order, stopping if any issue fails. Use this when later issues depend on earlier ones.
    42→
    43→### Preview with Dry Run
    44→
    45→```bash
    46→npx sequant run 100 --dry-run
    47→```
    48→
    49→Shows what would be executed without actually running any phases. Useful for verifying configuration.
    50→
    51→## Options & Settings
    52→
    53→| Option | Description | Default |
    54→|--------|-------------|---------|
    55→| `--phases <list>` | Comma-separated phases to run | `spec,exec,qa` |
    56→| `--sequential` | Run issues in order, stop on first failure (see [Execution Model](#execution-model)) | `false` |
    57→| `--chain` | Chain issues: each branches from previous (implies `--sequential`) | `false` |
    58→| `--qa-gate` | Wait for QA pass before starting next issue (requires `--chain`) | `false` |
    59→| `-d, --dry-run` | Preview without execution | `false` |
    60→| `-v, --verbose` | Show detailed output | `false` |
    61→| `--timeout <seconds>` | Timeout per phase | `1800` (30 min) |
    62→| `-q, --quality-loop` | Enable auto-retry on failures | `false` |
    63→| `--max-iterations <n>` | Max iterations for quality loop | `3` |
    64→| `--testgen` | Run testgen phase after spec | `false` |
    65→| `--batch "<issues>"` | Group issues to run together | - |
    66→| `--no-mcp` | Disable MCP servers for faster/cheaper runs | `false` |
    67→
    68→### Available Phases
    69→
    70→| Phase | Description |
    71→|-------|-------------|
    72→| `spec` | Planning and specification review |
    73→| `testgen` | Generate test stubs from spec |
    74→| `exec` | Implementation execution |
    75→| `test` | Run tests and verify |
    76→| `qa` | Quality review and approval |
    77→| `loop` | Quality iteration loop |
    78→
    79→## Execution Model
    80→
    81→Issues are always processed **one at a time** (serially). The `--sequential` flag controls **failure behavior**, not concurrency:
    82→
    83→| Mode | Flag | Behavior on Failure |
    84→|------|------|---------------------|
    85→| Default | _(none)_ | Continue to next issue |
    86→| Sequential | `--sequential` | Stop immediately |
    87→
    88→**Why not concurrent?** The Claude Agent SDK processes one agent session at a time. True concurrent execution (e.g., via listr2) is a potential future enhancement, but the current architecture runs issues serially regardless of the `--sequential` flag.
    89→
    90→**What `--sequential` actually controls:**
    91→
    92→```bash
    93→# Default: process all issues, continue if #101 fails
    94→npx sequant run 100 101 102
    95→#   ✓ #100 → ✗ #101 → ✓ #102  (all attempted)
    96→
    97→# Sequential: stop on first failure
    98→npx sequant run 100 101 102 --sequential
    99→#   ✓ #100 → ✗ #101  (stopped, #102 skipped)
   100→```

1→# Two Modes of Operation
     2→
     3→Sequant offers two ways to work: interactive in Claude Code chat, or autonomous via headless CLI.
     4→
     5→## Mode Comparison
     6→
     7→| Aspect | Interactive | Autonomous |
     8→|--------|-------------|------------|
     9→| **Interface** | Claude Code chat | Terminal CLI |
    10→| **Control** | Review each phase | Hands-off |
    11→| **Best for** | Complex issues, learning | Batch processing |
    12→| **Iteration** | Manual `/loop` | Automatic quality loop |
    13→
    14→## Interactive Mode
    15→
    16→Use slash commands in Claude Code chat for step-by-step control.
    17→
    18→### Commands
    19→
    20→```bash
    21→/spec 123      # Plan implementation
    22→/exec 123      # Build in worktree
    23→/test 123      # UI verification
    24→/qa 123        # Quality review
    25→/loop 123      # Fix and retry
    26→
    27→# Or all-in-one
    28→/fullsolve 123
    29→```
    30→
    31→### When to Use
    32→
    33→- **Learning Sequant** — See each phase's output
    34→- **Complex issues** — Review plans before implementation
    35→- **Sensitive changes** — Need human oversight at each step
    36→- **Debugging** — Investigate issues between phases
    37→
    38→### Example Session
    39→
    40→```
    41→You: /spec 42
    42→
    43→Claude: I've analyzed issue #42 and drafted a plan...
    44→[Shows implementation plan]
    45→
    46→You: Looks good, but let's use a different approach for the database query.
    47→
    48→Claude: Updated plan. Ready for /exec?
    49→
    50→You: /exec 42
    51→
    52→Claude: Implemented in worktree feature/42-add-caching...
    53→[Shows changes and test results]
    54→
    55→You: /qa 42
    56→
    57→Claude: QA complete. All AC items satisfied. Ready to merge.
    58→```
    59→
    60→### Advantages
    61→
    62→- Full visibility into each phase
    63→- Can intervene and redirect
    64→- Better for learning and understanding
    65→- Collaborative decision-making
    66→
    67→## Autonomous Mode
    68→
    69→Use the CLI for batch processing without interaction.
    70→
    71→### Commands
    72→
    73→```bash
    74→# Single issue
    75→npx sequant run 123
    76→
    77→# Multiple issues in parallel
    78→npx sequant run 1 2 3
    79→
    80→# Sequential processing
    81→npx sequant run 1 2 3 --sequential
    82→
    83→# With quality loop (auto-fix)
    84→npx sequant run 123 --quality-loop
    85→
    86→# Custom phases
    87→npx sequant run 123 --phases exec,qa
    88→
    89→# Preview without execution
    90→npx sequant run 123 --dry-run
    91→```
    92→
    93→### When to Use
    94→
    95→- **Batch processing** — Multiple issues at once
    96→- **CI/CD integration** — Automated workflows
    97→- **Simple issues** — Bug fixes, docs updates
    98→- **Overnight runs** — Queue work and review later
    99→
   100→### Example: Batch Processing
   101→
   102→```bash
   103→# Process sprint backlog overnight
   104→npx sequant run 45 46 47 48 --quality-loop
   105→
   106→# In the morning, review PRs
   107→gh pr list
   108→```
   109→
   110→### Quality Loop
   111→
   112→Autonomous mode can automatically fix issues:
   113→
   114→```bash
   115→npx sequant run 123 --quality-loop --max-iterations 5
   116→```
   117→
   118→**How it works:**
   119→1. Runs phases (spec → exec → qa)
   120→2. If QA fails, runs `/loop` to fix
   121→3. Re-runs failed phases
   122→4. Repeats up to N iterations
   123→
   124→### Smart Defaults
   125→
   126→Quality loop auto-enables for issues with these labels:
   127→- `complex`
   128→- `refactor`
   129→- `breaking`
   130→- `major`
   131→
   132→### Advantages
   133→
   134→- Process multiple issues efficiently
   135→- No manual intervention needed
   136→- Consistent execution
   137→- Good for overnight batch work
   138→
   139→## Hybrid Workflow
   140→
   141→Combine modes for the best of both:
   142→
   143→```bash
   144→# Start with interactive spec
   145→/spec 123
   146→# Review and approve plan
   147→
   148→# Run implementation autonomously
   149→npx sequant run 123 --phases exec,qa --quality-loop
   150→
   151→# Review results in chat
   152→/assess 123
   153→```
   154→
   155→## Mode Selection Guide
   156→
   157→| Scenario | Recommended Mode |
   158→|----------|------------------|
   159→| First time using Sequant | Interactive |
   160→| Complex feature with unclear requirements | Interactive |
   161→| Simple bug fix | Autonomous |
   162→| Sprint batch processing | Autonomous |
   163→| Security-sensitive change | Interactive |
   164→| Documentation updates | Autonomous |
   165→| Refactoring with breaking changes | Interactive (spec), then autonomous |
   166→
   167→## Configuration
   168→
   169→### Default Settings
   170→
   171→Configure in `.sequant/settings.json`:
   172→
   173→```json
   174→{
   175→  "run": {
   176→    "qualityLoop": false,
   177→    "maxIterations": 3,
   178→    "sequential": false
   179→  }
   180→}
   181→```
   182→
   183→### Override Per Run
   184→
   185→```bash
   186→# Override settings with flags
   187→npx sequant run 123 --quality-loop --max-iterations 5 --sequential
   188→```
   189→
   190→CLI flags always override settings file values.
   191→
